title: Policies for Information Security
description: A set of policies for information security shall be defined, approved by management, published and communicated to employees and relevant external parties.
purpose: To provide management direction and support for information security in accordance with business requirements and relevant laws and regulations.
implementationGuidance: Policies should be documented and cover the overall direction and principles for protecting information. They should be approved at the highest appropriate management level. Policies must be communicated to relevant personnel and reviewed at planned intervals or when significant changes occur.
evidenceExamples: Email distribution records showing policy communication
bestPractices: Review policy annually or after significant changes
Align with ISO 27001 clauses 5.2 and 6.2
Keep the policy concise and high-level; detail goes in supporting procedures

title: Review of the Policies for Information Security
description: The policies for information security shall be reviewed at planned intervals or if significant changes occur to ensure their continuing suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness.
purpose: To ensure information security policies remain relevant and effective as the organisation and threat landscape evolve.
implementationGuidance: Establish a documented review cycle (typically annual). Reviews should assess policy adequacy in relation to changes in the organisation's risk profile, technology, legislation, and business environment. Document review outcomes including any changes made.
bestPractices: Trigger reviews after security incidents or major organisational changes
Involve senior management and legal counsel in reviews
Track version control with dates of approval

title: Information Security Roles and Responsibilities
description: All information security responsibilities shall be defined and allocated.
purpose: To establish a clear governance structure for information security across the organisation.
implementationGuidance: Define roles such as CISO, Information Security Manager, Data Owner, System Administrator, and end-user. Document responsibilities for protecting assets and implementing specific security processes. Responsibilities for carrying out security processes and reporting security incidents must be clearly allocated.
bestPractices: Appoint a dedicated Information Security Manager or CISO
Ensure roles are proportionate to the organisation's risk profile
Review and update role assignments when personnel changes occur

title: Segregation of Duties
description: Conflicting duties and areas of responsibility shall be segregated to reduce opportunities for unauthorised or unintentional modification or misuse of the organisation's assets.
purpose: To reduce the risk of error, fraud, or misuse of information assets through conflict of interest.
implementationGuidance: Identify duties that require segregation (e.g., requesting vs. approving access, developing vs. deploying code). Where full segregation is not feasible, implement compensating controls such as management oversight, audit trails, and monitoring.
evidenceExamples: Access control list showing separated privileges
bestPractices: Automate segregation checks in IT systems where possible
Review segregation arrangements annually
Document compensating controls where full separation is impractical

title: Contact with Authorities
description: Appropriate contacts with relevant authorities shall be maintained.
purpose: To ensure the organisation can respond effectively to legal, regulatory, and law enforcement requirements.
implementationGuidance: Identify the relevant authorities such as national cybersecurity agencies, data protection authorities, law enforcement, and regulators. Document contact details and the circumstances under which contact should be made. Designate who is responsible for liaising with authorities.
evidenceExamples: Incident response runbook with authority notification steps
bestPractices: Include regulatory notification requirements in incident response plans
Keep authority contact lists current and accessible
Train incident response team on when and how to contact authorities

title: Contact with Special Interest Groups
description: Appropriate contacts with special interest groups or other specialist security forums and professional associations shall be maintained.
purpose: To stay informed of the latest threat intelligence, security advisories, and industry best practices.
implementationGuidance: Participate in relevant information sharing organisations (e.g., ISACs, CERT networks, CISA advisories). Subscribe to vendor security bulletins. Document memberships and the channels through which threat intelligence is received and acted upon.
requiredDocuments: Process for acting on security advisories
evidenceExamples: Records of actions taken on security advisories
bestPractices: Join sector-specific ISACs
Assign responsibility for monitoring and acting on threat intelligence
Share relevant intelligence within the organisation promptly

title: Information Security in Project Management
description: Information security shall be addressed in project management, regardless of the type of project.
purpose: To ensure that security considerations are integrated into projects from inception rather than bolted on after completion.
implementationGuidance: Incorporate information security checkpoints into the project lifecycle (initiation, design, development, testing, deployment). Conduct security risk assessments for projects handling personal or sensitive data. Require security sign-off as part of project governance.
requiredDocuments: Security risk assessment template for projects
evidenceExamples: Security assessment reports for active projects
Security gate approvals in project documentation
bestPractices: Embed security requirements in project initiation documents
Include CISO or security team in project steering committees
Conduct security testing before go-live

title: Mobile Device Policy
description: A policy and supporting security measures shall be adopted to manage the risks introduced by using mobile devices.
purpose: To protect information accessed or processed on mobile devices against loss, theft, and unauthorised access.
implementationGuidance: Define acceptable use of mobile devices for corporate purposes. Require device encryption, strong authentication (PIN/biometric), and remote wipe capability. Address bring-your-own-device (BYOD) vs. corporate-owned policies. Implement Mobile Device Management (MDM) for enrolled devices.
evidenceExamples: MDM console showing enrolled device compliance
Signed mobile device acceptable use agreements
bestPractices: Mandate full disk encryption on all mobile devices
Use containerisation to separate corporate and personal data on BYOD
Automate compliance checks via MDM

title: Teleworking
description: A policy and supporting security measures shall be implemented to protect information accessed, processed or stored at teleworking sites.
purpose: To ensure information remains secure when employees work remotely from the traditional office environment.
implementationGuidance: Define requirements for home offices and remote work environments including secure Wi-Fi, VPN usage, and clean desk principles. Address the use of public networks. Specify requirements for accessing corporate systems remotely.
bestPractices: Require VPN for all remote access to corporate systems
Conduct periodic checks on remote worker compliance
Provide guidance on securing home Wi-Fi networks

title: Screening
description: Background verification checks on all candidates for employment shall be carried out in accordance with relevant laws, regulations and ethics and shall be proportional to the business requirements, the classification of the information to be accessed and the perceived risks.
purpose: To ensure employees, contractors, and third-party users are suitable and trustworthy prior to being granted access to information assets.
implementationGuidance: Define screening requirements proportionate to the sensitivity of roles (e.g., criminal record checks, employment history verification, qualification checks, credit checks for financial roles). Comply with local employment law and data protection regulations.
bestPractices: Re-screen employees moving to higher-sensitivity roles
Use accredited third-party screening services
Document the risk-based rationale for each level of screening

title: Terms and Conditions of Employment
description: The contractual agreements with employees and contractors shall state their and the organisation's responsibilities for information security.
purpose: To ensure all personnel understand their information security obligations before being granted access to organisational information.
implementationGuidance: Include information security responsibilities in employment contracts and contractor agreements. Reference relevant policies employees are required to comply with. Include clauses on confidentiality, acceptable use, data handling, and consequences of non-compliance.
requiredDocuments: Employment contract template with security clauses
bestPractices: Include security obligations in both employment and contractor contracts
Require fresh signatures when policies are significantly updated
Clearly state consequences for violation

title: Management Responsibilities
description: Management shall require all employees and contractors to apply information security in accordance with the established policies and procedures of the organisation.
purpose: To ensure management actively enforces information security policies and sets the right example for their teams.
implementationGuidance: Include information security responsibilities in manager job descriptions. Require managers to ensure their team members complete mandatory security training and comply with policies. Incorporate security compliance into performance management.
evidenceExamples: Manager job descriptions with security responsibilities
Team training completion reports reviewed by managers
bestPractices: Hold managers accountable for their team's security compliance
Provide managers with regular reports on their team's compliance status
Include security in leadership training programmes

title: Information Security Awareness, Education and Training
description: All employees of the organisation and, where relevant, contractors shall receive appropriate awareness education and training and regular updates in organisational policies and procedures.
purpose: To ensure all personnel are aware of information security threats and their responsibilities to protect information.
implementationGuidance: Deliver security awareness training at onboarding and annually thereafter. Include role-specific training (e.g., developer security training, phishing awareness). Track completion rates and test knowledge retention.
bestPractices: Run phishing simulations quarterly
Make training engaging and role-relevant
Test comprehension with quizzes and track pass rates

title: Disciplinary Process
description: There shall be a formal and communicated disciplinary process in place to take action against employees who have committed an information security breach.
purpose: To deter and respond appropriately to intentional or negligent violations of information security policies.
implementationGuidance: Document a disciplinary process that is proportionate to the nature and severity of the security violation. Ensure the process is fair, legally compliant, and consistently applied. Communicate the process to all staff.
requiredDocuments: Disciplinary policy referencing information security violations
evidenceExamples: Training records showing policy has been communicated
bestPractices: Align disciplinary process with employment law
Treat security violations consistently and proportionately
Use disciplinary actions as learning opportunities with broader communication

title: Termination or Change of Employment Responsibilities
description: Information security responsibilities and duties that remain valid after termination or change of employment shall be defined, communicated to the employee or contractor and enforced.
purpose: To protect the organisation's information assets when the employment relationship changes or ends.
implementationGuidance: Define a joiners/movers/leavers process that includes timely revocation of access rights, return of assets, and completion of exit security procedures. Remind departing employees of ongoing confidentiality obligations.
evidenceExamples: Access revocation tickets closed on or before last working day
bestPractices: Automate account deactivation on the last day of employment
Conduct exit interviews to identify security risks
Review and update access immediately upon role changes

title: Inventory of Assets
description: Assets associated with information and information processing facilities shall be identified and an inventory of these assets shall be drawn up and maintained.
purpose: To ensure that all information assets are identified and appropriately owned so that they can be protected.
implementationGuidance: Maintain a current inventory of information assets including hardware, software, data repositories, and services. Include asset owner, custodian, location, classification, and lifecycle status. Update the inventory as assets are acquired, changed, or decommissioned.
bestPractices: Use automated discovery tools to keep the inventory current
Integrate asset management with the CMDB
Review and reconcile the inventory at least annually

title: Ownership of Assets
description: Assets maintained in the inventory shall be owned.
purpose: To ensure accountability for the protection and appropriate use of every information asset.
implementationGuidance: Assign a named owner (individual or role) for each asset in the inventory. The owner is responsible for the appropriate classification, protection, and access control for their assets. Define what asset ownership entails in the asset management policy.
requiredDocuments: Asset management policy defining owner responsibilities
bestPractices: Assign ownership to business roles, not individuals, to survive staff turnover
Automate owner reminders to review their assets annually
Clearly document what ownership responsibilities entail

title: Acceptable Use of Assets
description: Rules for the acceptable use of information and of assets associated with information and information processing facilities shall be identified, documented and implemented.
purpose: To define and communicate standards of appropriate asset use to all personnel.
implementationGuidance: Create an Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) covering corporate devices, email, internet, cloud services, and company data. Communicate the AUP to all users and obtain signed acknowledgement. Include in onboarding.
bestPractices: Write the AUP in plain language accessible to all staff
Update the AUP annually or when significant technology changes occur
Monitor for AUP violations using technical controls

title: Return of Assets
description: All employees and external party users shall return all of the organisational assets in their possession upon termination of their employment, contract or agreement.
purpose: To prevent loss of organisational assets when employment or contractual relationships end.
implementationGuidance: Define a process requiring return of all physical (laptops, tokens, badges) and logical (access credentials, data copies) assets at offboarding. Include on the offboarding checklist. Track and enforce compliance.
requiredDocuments: Offboarding checklist with asset return items
bestPractices: Initiate asset return process before the final day where possible
Use signed loan agreements for corporate assets
Follow up promptly for any assets not returned

title: Classification of Information
description: Information shall be classified in terms of legal requirements, value, criticality and sensitivity to unauthorised disclosure or modification.
purpose: To ensure that information receives an appropriate level of protection according to its importance to the organisation.
implementationGuidance: Establish an information classification scheme (e.g., Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted). Define criteria for each classification level. Train staff on how to classify information they create or handle.
bestPractices: Keep the classification scheme simple — typically 3–4 levels
Default to a higher classification when uncertain
Automate classification in document management systems where possible

title: Labelling of Information
description: An appropriate set of procedures for information labelling shall be developed and implemented in accordance with the information classification scheme adopted by the organisation.
purpose: To make information classification visible so that appropriate handling procedures can be applied.
implementationGuidance: Define labelling requirements for different media types (electronic documents, email, physical documents, removable media). Implement automated labelling tools where possible. Ensure labels are clearly visible and meaningful to users.
bestPractices: Automate labelling using tools like Microsoft Purview Information Protection
Include classification labels in document templates
Audit labelling compliance periodically

title: Handling of Assets
description: Procedures for handling assets shall be developed and implemented in accordance with the information classification scheme adopted by the organisation.
purpose: To ensure information assets are protected appropriately based on their classification throughout their lifecycle.
implementationGuidance: Define handling procedures for each classification level covering storage, transmission, disposal, and access. Include procedures for different media types. Ensure procedures address both digital and physical assets.
requiredDocuments: Asset handling procedures per classification level
evidenceExamples: Encrypted storage evidence for Confidential data
bestPractices: Implement technical controls to enforce handling requirements automatically
Provide clear guidance for common scenarios (e.g., emailing confidential data)
Review handling procedures when the classification scheme changes

title: Management of Removable Media
description: Procedures shall be implemented for the management of removable media in accordance with the classification scheme adopted by the organisation.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised disclosure or loss of information stored on removable media.
implementationGuidance: Define policy on the use of removable media (USB drives, external hard disks, CDs). Consider restricting or prohibiting use where not operationally required. Where permitted, require encryption and logging of data transfers.
requiredDocuments: Acceptable use procedures for removable media
evidenceExamples: Technical controls blocking unauthorised USB usage
bestPractices: Use endpoint DLP solutions to control and log removable media usage
Encrypt all removable media containing sensitive data
Maintain an inventory of corporate-issued removable media

title: Disposal of Media
description: Media shall be disposed of securely when no longer required, using formal procedures.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised disclosure of information from media that is no longer in use.
implementationGuidance: Implement secure disposal procedures for all media types including hard drives, tapes, CDs, mobile devices, and paper. Use certified data destruction methods (e.g., DoD 5220.22-M, NIST 800-88). Maintain records of all media disposed.
evidenceExamples: Certificates of destruction from approved vendors
Internal disposal records for physical media
Hard drive degaussing or shredding records
bestPractices: Use certified third-party data destruction services for physical media
Follow NIST SP 800-88 guidelines for sanitisation
Keep a disposal log with asset ID, method, and witness/certificate

title: Physical Media Transfer
description: Media containing information shall be protected against unauthorised access, misuse or corruption during transportation.
purpose: To protect information carried on physical media during transit between locations.
implementationGuidance: Define procedures for transporting physical media containing sensitive or classified information. Use reliable couriers, tamper-evident packaging, and encryption. Maintain chain-of-custody records.
bestPractices: Prefer electronic transfer over physical media where possible
Encrypt data before physical transfer regardless of classification
Use trackable couriers with proof of delivery

title: Access Control Policy
description: An access control policy shall be established, documented and reviewed based on business and information security requirements.
purpose: To ensure that access to information systems and data is appropriately controlled and aligned with business needs.
implementationGuidance: Document an access control policy that defines principles (need-to-know, least privilege), roles and responsibilities, and requirements for managing user access. Review the policy annually and when significant changes occur.
evidenceExamples: Approved and published access control policy
RBAC matrix showing access levels by role
bestPractices: Apply the principle of least privilege across all systems
Implement RBAC for consistent access management
Review access control policy after significant system or organisational changes

title: Access to Networks and Network Services
description: Users shall only be provided with access to the network and network services that they have been specifically authorised to use.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised access to networked services and resources.
implementationGuidance: Define which network services are accessible to different user roles. Implement network segmentation and firewall rules to enforce access restrictions. Use VPN and MFA for remote access. Maintain documentation of authorised network access.
evidenceExamples: Firewall rule sets aligned to policy
Network access request and approval records
bestPractices: Segment networks by sensitivity level (e.g., PCI CDE, production, development)
Implement zero-trust network access principles
Review firewall rules quarterly to remove unnecessary access

title: User Registration and De-registration
description: A formal user registration and de-registration process shall be implemented to enable assignment of access rights.
purpose: To ensure a controlled process for granting and revoking system access tied to the employment lifecycle.
implementationGuidance: Implement a formal process for creating user accounts tied to HR joiners/leavers workflow. Require authorised requests for account creation. Ensure accounts are deactivated promptly on termination. Maintain an audit trail of all account changes.
evidenceExamples: Account creation request tickets with approval
bestPractices: Automate account provisioning and deprovisioning through HR system integration
Disable accounts immediately upon termination, delete after a retention period
Conduct regular audits of active accounts vs. current employee list

title: User Access Provisioning
description: A formal user access provisioning process shall be implemented to assign or revoke access rights for all user types to all systems and services.
purpose: To ensure users receive only the access they need for their role and that access is formally approved.
implementationGuidance: Implement a formal access request, approval, and provisioning workflow. Require business owner approval for access to sensitive systems. Document access granted for each user. Review access when roles change.
evidenceExamples: Access request tickets with manager approval
Access matrix updated upon role changes
bestPractices: Use an Identity Governance and Administration (IGA) tool for scalable provisioning
Require dual approval for privileged access provisioning
Automatically trigger access review when a user changes roles

title: Management of Privileged Access Rights
description: The allocation and use of privileged access rights shall be restricted and controlled.
purpose: To minimise the risk of misuse of privileged accounts that can bypass normal controls.
implementationGuidance: Restrict privileged accounts to the minimum necessary users. Require additional justification and approval for privileged access. Implement Privileged Access Management (PAM) solutions. Log and monitor all privileged activity. Review privileged access regularly.
bestPractices: Implement just-in-time (JIT) privileged access
Use a PAM solution to vault and manage privileged credentials
Require MFA for all privileged access
Review privileged accounts at least quarterly

title: Management of Secret Authentication Information of Users
description: The allocation of secret authentication information shall be controlled through a formal management process.
purpose: To ensure authentication credentials are distributed and managed securely.
implementationGuidance: Require secure initial password distribution (e.g., requiring immediate change on first use). Prohibit sharing of passwords. Enforce password complexity and rotation requirements. Consider passwordless authentication methods.
evidenceExamples: Password policy configuration in identity provider
bestPractices: Deploy an enterprise password manager
Enforce MFA alongside password requirements
Consider moving to passwordless authentication where feasible

title: Review of User Access Rights
description: Asset owners shall review users' access rights at regular intervals.
purpose: To ensure access rights remain appropriate and are revoked when no longer needed.
implementationGuidance: Conduct formal access reviews at least every 6 months (more frequently for privileged access). Business owners review access to their systems. Document review outcomes and revoke inappropriate access promptly.
evidenceExamples: Completed access review reports with owner sign-off
Access revocation tickets raised as result of review
bestPractices: Automate access review campaigns using IGA tooling
Prioritise reviews for critical systems and privileged accounts
Track and remediate findings from access reviews within defined SLAs

title: Removal or Adjustment of Access Rights
description: The access rights of all employees and external party users to information and information processing facilities shall be removed upon termination of their employment, contract or agreement, or adjusted upon change.
purpose: To ensure access rights are promptly revoked or modified when the employment relationship changes.
implementationGuidance: Define SLAs for access removal (e.g., immediately on termination, within 24 hours of role change). Integrate access revocation with the HR system. Verify access removal as part of the offboarding checklist.
evidenceExamples: Account deactivation logs tied to offboarding dates
bestPractices: Automate account deactivation via HR system integration
Audit all accounts quarterly against current employee list
Define escalation process for access not removed within SLA

title: Use of Secret Authentication Information
description: Users shall be required to follow the organisation's practices in the use of secret authentication information.
purpose: To ensure users handle passwords and other credentials securely to prevent unauthorised access.
implementationGuidance: Train users on password security best practices. Prohibit writing down passwords insecurely, sharing credentials, or using the same password across systems. Require use of corporate password manager.
requiredDocuments: Password use guidelines for end users
Acceptable use policy referencing password requirements
evidenceExamples: Security awareness training completion records covering passwords
bestPractices: Mandate use of a corporate password manager
Educate users to never share credentials under any circumstances
Run phishing simulations to test credential protection awareness

title: Information Access Restriction
description: Access to information and application system functions shall be restricted in accordance with the access control policy.
purpose: To ensure users can only access information required for their legitimate business role.
implementationGuidance: Implement technical access controls aligned to the access control policy. Use RBAC, attribute-based access control (ABAC), or mandatory access control (MAC) as appropriate. Enforce need-to-know restrictions within applications.
bestPractices: Implement access controls at multiple layers (application, database, OS)
Use attribute-based controls for fine-grained access to sensitive data
Test access controls during development and after configuration changes

title: Secure Log-on Procedures
description: Where required by the access control policy, access to systems and applications shall be controlled by a secure log-on procedure.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised access through weak or easily bypassed authentication mechanisms.
implementationGuidance: Implement secure log-on that limits failed login attempts, does not provide helpful error messages for invalid credentials, and logs successful and failed log-on attempts. Require MFA for sensitive systems. Display only necessary information before successful log-on.
evidenceExamples: MFA enforcement evidence in IAM system
bestPractices: Enforce MFA on all systems handling sensitive data
Implement account lockout after a defined number of failed attempts
Use CAPTCHA or similar controls on publicly facing log-on pages

title: Password Management System
description: Password management systems shall be interactive and shall ensure quality passwords.
purpose: To ensure technical controls enforce strong password standards and protect credentials.
implementationGuidance: Configure the password management system to enforce minimum complexity, prohibit reuse of recent passwords, require regular rotation (where appropriate), and securely hash stored passwords using modern algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2).
evidenceExamples: IAM system password policy configuration screenshots
bestPractices: Store passwords using bcrypt or Argon2 — never MD5 or SHA-1
Implement HIBP (Have I Been Pwned) checking for compromised passwords
Consider moving to passkeys or FIDO2 for higher security

title: Use of Privileged Utility Programs
description: The use of utility programs that might be capable of overriding system and application controls shall be restricted and tightly controlled.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised access or damage using powerful system utilities.
implementationGuidance: Maintain an inventory of privileged utility programs (e.g., system administration tools, network scanners, forensic tools). Restrict access to authorised personnel only. Log all use of such tools. Consider application whitelisting.
evidenceExamples: Audit logs of utility program usage
bestPractices: Implement application control (whitelisting) to prevent unauthorised tool execution
Log and alert on use of privileged utilities
Review and update the authorised utility list regularly

title: Access Control to Program Source Code
description: Access to program source code shall be restricted.
purpose: To protect intellectual property and prevent unauthorised modification of software.
implementationGuidance: Restrict source code access to authorised developers only. Use a version control system (e.g., Git) with access controls. Separate production code from development. Log all access to and changes of source code.
requiredDocuments: Code review and merge approval procedures
evidenceExamples: Source control system access permissions report
Code review audit trail (pull request approvals)
Developer access list for code repositories
bestPractices: Require code review (pull request approval) before any merge to main branches
Implement branch protection rules
Audit source code access quarterly

title: Policy on the Use of Cryptographic Controls
description: A policy on the use of cryptographic controls for protection of information shall be developed and implemented.
purpose: To ensure cryptography is used appropriately and consistently to protect information confidentiality and integrity.
implementationGuidance: Document a cryptography policy specifying approved algorithms, key lengths, and use cases. Address encryption at rest, in transit, and for specific data types. Define approved cipher suites and deprecate weak algorithms (DES, RC4, MD5).
requiredDocuments: Approved algorithms and key lengths list
Encryption standards for data at rest and in transit
evidenceExamples: Policy document specifying approved cryptographic algorithms
TLS configuration evidence showing approved cipher suites
Database and storage encryption configuration records
bestPractices: Use AES-256 for data at rest and TLS 1.2/1.3 for data in transit
Formally deprecate weak algorithms (DES, 3DES, RC4, MD5, SHA-1)
Review cryptographic standards annually against current NIST guidance

title: Key Management
description: A policy on the use, protection and lifetime of cryptographic keys shall be developed and implemented through their whole lifecycle.
purpose: To ensure cryptographic keys are generated, stored, distributed, retired, and destroyed securely to maintain the effectiveness of encryption.
implementationGuidance: Define key management procedures covering key generation (using approved random number generators), storage (HSMs or key management services), distribution (secure channels), rotation schedules, and destruction. Prevent key exposure.
bestPractices: Use a dedicated Key Management Service (KMS) or HSM
Define and automate key rotation schedules
Never store encryption keys alongside the data they protect

title: Physical Security Perimeter
description: Security perimeters shall be defined and used to protect areas that contain either sensitive or critical information and information processing facilities.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised physical access to organisational premises and facilities.
implementationGuidance: Define physical security zones (e.g., public, controlled, restricted, secure). Implement appropriate physical barriers for each zone (doors, walls, fences). Ensure security perimeters are clearly defined, solid, and approved by management.
evidenceExamples: Site security plan with zone definitions
CCTV and access control coverage maps
bestPractices: Implement layered physical security (multiple perimeter zones)
Ensure all entry/exit points are controlled and monitored
Conduct annual physical security assessments

title: Physical Entry Controls
description: Secure areas shall be protected by appropriate entry controls to ensure that only authorised personnel are allowed access.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised individuals from entering secure areas where sensitive assets or systems reside.
implementationGuidance: Implement entry controls such as access cards, PIN pads, biometrics, or security guards for secure areas. Log all access events. Ensure visitors are escorted and their access is controlled. Manage access card/badge lifecycle.
evidenceExamples: Access badge issuance and revocation records
bestPractices: Use multi-factor physical access (card + PIN) for critical areas
Review physical access rights quarterly
Implement anti-tailgating measures for high-security zones

title: Securing Offices, Rooms and Facilities
description: Physical security for offices, rooms, and facilities shall be designed and applied.
purpose: To protect organisational assets and information from physical threats within facilities.
implementationGuidance: Apply physical security measures to all offices housing sensitive systems or information. Secure server rooms, communications rooms, and data processing areas with additional controls. Consider security of meeting rooms where confidential discussions occur.
bestPractices: Require double authentication for server rooms
Ensure all server room access is logged and reviewed
Conduct regular physical security walkthroughs

title: Protecting Against External and Environmental Threats
description: Physical protection against natural disasters, malicious attack or accidents shall be designed and applied.
purpose: To protect facilities and information assets from physical threats such as fire, flood, earthquake, and deliberate attack.
implementationGuidance: Identify environmental risks (flood, fire, earthquake) and implement appropriate protections. Install fire suppression, flood barriers, and earthquake-resistant racks. Avoid locating critical facilities in areas prone to natural disasters where possible.
requiredDocuments: Business continuity plan referencing physical threats
bestPractices: Conduct annual environmental risk assessments
Test fire suppression systems regularly
Ensure critical systems are replicated at a geographically separate location

title: Working in Secure Areas
description: Procedures for working in secure areas shall be designed and applied.
purpose: To prevent security incidents arising from work conducted within secure areas.
implementationGuidance: Define procedures for activities within secure areas (e.g., server rooms, secure offices). Prohibit unaccompanied visits, personal devices unless authorised, and unnecessary access. Consider monitoring secure area activity.
evidenceExamples: Visitor escort records for secure areas
Device usage logs in secure areas
bestPractices: Require escort for all visitors in secure areas
Prohibit photography in secure areas
Conduct regular unannounced inspections of secure area compliance

title: Delivery and Loading Areas
description: Access points such as delivery and loading areas and other points where unauthorised persons could enter the premises shall be controlled and, if possible, isolated from information processing facilities to avoid unauthorised access.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised access to organisational premises through delivery and loading areas.
implementationGuidance: Control access to delivery and loading areas. Ensure deliveries are logged and inspected. Prevent direct access from delivery areas to sensitive parts of the facility. Require security personnel to supervise deliveries.
evidenceExamples: Delivery area CCTV footage review records
bestPractices: Keep delivery areas separated from processing areas by security zones
Log and inspect all incoming deliveries
Use CCTV coverage for all delivery and loading areas

title: Equipment Siting and Protection
description: Equipment shall be sited and protected to reduce the risks from environmental threats and hazards, and opportunities for unauthorised access.
purpose: To protect IT equipment from physical damage, theft, and eavesdropping.
implementationGuidance: Site equipment to minimise risks from environmental factors (heat, humidity, water, power fluctuations). Restrict physical access to critical equipment. Ensure sensitive display screens are not visible to unauthorised parties.
bestPractices: Use hot/cold aisle containment in data centres for temperature management
Implement screen privacy filters for sensitive workstations in open areas
Ensure equipment is anchored to prevent removal

title: Supporting Utilities
description: Equipment shall be protected from power failures and other disruptions caused by failures in supporting utilities.
purpose: To ensure continuous availability of IT systems by protecting against power and utility failures.
implementationGuidance: Implement UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supplies) for critical systems. Install backup generators for extended outages. Ensure redundant power feeds and environmental controls (HVAC). Conduct regular tests of backup power systems.
evidenceExamples: UPS test records showing successful failover
bestPractices: Test UPS and generators quarterly under load
Ensure HVAC has backup capability for extended power outages
Monitor environmental conditions (power, temperature, humidity) with automated alerts

title: Cabling Security
description: Power and telecommunications cabling carrying data or supporting information services shall be protected from interception, interference or damage.
purpose: To protect network and power cabling from physical tampering, damage, or eavesdropping.
implementationGuidance: Route network cabling away from public areas and areas with potential for damage. Label and document all network cabling. Protect sensitive network cabling in conduits. Consider fibre optic cabling for high-security environments due to its resistance to electromagnetic interference and eavesdropping.
evidenceExamples: Conduit and cable management installation evidence
bestPractices: Implement structured cabling with comprehensive documentation
Inspect cabling during physical security audits
Use fibre optic cabling in high-security or high-risk areas

title: Equipment Maintenance
description: Equipment shall be correctly maintained to ensure its continued availability and integrity.
purpose: To ensure equipment remains in good working condition and reduces the risk of system failure.
implementationGuidance: Implement a preventive maintenance programme for all critical equipment. Follow manufacturer maintenance schedules. Conduct maintenance only by authorised personnel. Document all maintenance activities.
bestPractices: Schedule preventive maintenance proactively rather than waiting for failure
Verify maintenance personnel identity and authorisation before granting access
Include equipment maintenance status in asset inventory

title: Removal of Assets
description: Equipment, information or software shall not be taken off-site without prior authorisation.
purpose: To prevent theft or loss of organisational assets removed from the premises.
implementationGuidance: Require formal authorisation for removing equipment, data, or software from organisational premises. Log all removals. Implement physical security measures to prevent unauthorised removal (e.g., cable locks, security checks at exits).
evidenceExamples: Security check records at building exits
bestPractices: Implement a formal checkout process for all asset removals
Use cable locks for portable equipment that cannot be removed
Conduct spot checks on items being removed from the premises

title: Security of Equipment and Assets Off-Premises
description: Security shall be applied to off-site assets taking into account the different risks of working outside the organisation's premises.
purpose: To ensure information assets are adequately protected when used outside the organisational premises.
implementationGuidance: Define security requirements for equipment used off-premises (encryption, secure storage, VPN usage, physical protection). Train users on responsibilities when using equipment outside the office. Require prompt reporting of lost or stolen equipment.
bestPractices: Require full disk encryption on all portable devices
Enable remote wipe capability on all mobile devices
Train users to never leave devices unattended in public places

title: Secure Disposal or Re-use of Equipment
description: All items of equipment containing storage media shall be verified to ensure that any sensitive data and licensed software has been removed or securely overwritten prior to disposal or re-use.
purpose: To prevent data leakage from equipment being disposed of or repurposed.
implementationGuidance: Implement secure data sanitisation before equipment disposal or re-use. Use NIST 800-88 guidelines for sanitisation methods appropriate to different media types. Document all sanitisation and disposal activities. Use certified destruction services.
evidenceExamples: Certificates of destruction from approved vendors
Disposal tracking records with asset IDs
bestPractices: Follow NIST SP 800-88 for media sanitisation
Use certified data destruction services and obtain certificates
Document the disposal chain of custody

title: Unattended User Equipment
description: Users shall ensure that unattended equipment has appropriate protection.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised access to systems and data when equipment is left unattended.
implementationGuidance: Configure automatic screen lock after a defined period of inactivity (e.g., 5–10 minutes). Train users to lock screens when stepping away. Implement clear screen policies for sensitive areas.
evidenceExamples: Clear screen policy published and communicated
Security awareness training covering screen locking
bestPractices: Set screen lock to activate after no more than 5 minutes of inactivity
Configure automatic logout for long-idle sessions
Use privacy screens for workstations in open areas

title: Clear Desk and Clear Screen Policy
description: A clear desk policy for papers and removable storage media and a clear screen policy for information processing facilities shall be adopted.
purpose: To reduce the risk of unauthorised access or loss of information from unattended desks and screens.
implementationGuidance: Implement a clear desk policy requiring sensitive documents to be stored securely when not in use. Require screen locking when leaving workstations. Conduct periodic clear desk audits. Include in security awareness training.
evidenceExamples: Policy published and signed by employees
bestPractices: Conduct surprise clear desk inspections
Provide lockable storage (cabinets, pedestals) for all employees
Include clear desk reminders in security awareness programmes

title: Documented Operating Procedures
description: Operating procedures shall be documented and made available to all users who need them.
purpose: To ensure consistent and correct operation of information processing facilities.
implementationGuidance: Document procedures for all operational activities including system startup, shutdown, error handling, backup, and maintenance. Maintain procedures in an accessible and version-controlled system. Review procedures at defined intervals.
evidenceExamples: Published IT runbooks with version control
Operations team access to procedure documentation
bestPractices: Store all procedures in a version-controlled knowledge base
Review procedures at least annually and after significant changes
Test procedures by having new staff follow them without assistance

title: Change Management
description: Changes to the organisation, business processes, information processing facilities and systems that affect information security shall be controlled.
purpose: To ensure changes are planned, tested, and authorised to prevent security incidents caused by uncontrolled changes.
implementationGuidance: Implement a formal change management process including change request, impact assessment, approval, scheduling, testing, rollback planning, and post-implementation review. Maintain a change log. Apply emergency change procedures for urgent situations.
evidenceExamples: Change tickets with full lifecycle documentation
CAB meeting minutes with change approvals
Emergency change records with post-implementation review
bestPractices: Use a recognised ITSM framework (e.g., ITIL) for change management
Automate change advisory workflows in your ITSM tool
Conduct post-implementation reviews for all significant changes

title: Capacity Management
description: The use of resources shall be monitored, tuned and projections made of future capacity requirements to ensure the required system performance.
purpose: To ensure systems have sufficient capacity to meet business needs and avoid performance-related availability incidents.
implementationGuidance: Monitor resource utilisation (CPU, memory, storage, network bandwidth) across critical systems. Set thresholds and alerts for capacity constraints. Conduct periodic capacity planning reviews and project future requirements.
bestPractices: Monitor capacity continuously and alert before thresholds are breached
Conduct formal capacity planning at least annually
Use cloud auto-scaling where possible to handle demand spikes

title: Separation of Development, Testing and Operational Environments
description: Development, testing, and operational environments shall be separated to reduce the risks of unauthorised access or changes to the operational environment.
purpose: To prevent untested changes or test data from impacting production systems and data.
implementationGuidance: Maintain separate environments for development, testing/QA, and production. Control access separately for each environment. Prohibit use of real personal data in development/test environments. Implement formal promotion procedures between environments.
evidenceExamples: Access control lists for each environment
bestPractices: Use infrastructure-as-code to ensure environment consistency and separation
Never copy production personal data to lower environments without anonymisation
Require separate approvals for promotions to production

title: Controls Against Malware
description: Detection, prevention and recovery controls to protect against malware shall be implemented, combined with appropriate user awareness.
purpose: To protect information systems and data from malware threats.
implementationGuidance: Deploy endpoint protection (antivirus/EDR) on all endpoints. Implement email and web filtering. Keep malware signatures and software up to date. Train users on recognising malware threats. Define incident response procedures for malware incidents.
evidenceExamples: Email filtering logs showing blocked malware
Security awareness training records covering malware
bestPractices: Deploy next-gen EDR rather than traditional signature-based AV
Implement application whitelisting for high-risk environments
Conduct regular phishing simulations as malware prevention training

title: Information Backup
description: Backup copies of information, software and system images shall be taken and tested regularly in accordance with an agreed backup policy.
purpose: To ensure information can be recovered in the event of data loss, corruption, or a disaster.
implementationGuidance: Define backup frequency, retention periods, and restoration requirements for all critical data. Implement automated backups. Store backups in a separate location (offsite or cloud). Test backup restoration regularly and document results.
evidenceExamples: Backup restoration test reports with results
bestPractices: Follow the 3-2-1 backup rule: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite
Test restoration at least quarterly; full restoration test annually
Verify backup integrity and not just backup completion

title: Event Logging
description: Event logs recording user activities, exceptions, faults and information security events shall be produced, kept and regularly reviewed.
purpose: To detect and investigate security events, policy violations, and system anomalies.
implementationGuidance: Define what events to log across systems (authentication, access, changes, errors). Centralise logs in a SIEM or log management platform. Define log retention periods aligned with regulatory requirements. Conduct regular log reviews.
bestPractices: Centralise all logs in a SIEM for correlation and alerting
Retain logs for at least 12 months with at least 3 months immediately accessible
Automate log review with SIEM alerting rules

title: Protection of Log Information
description: Logging facilities and log information shall be protected against tampering and unauthorised access.
purpose: To maintain the integrity and availability of log data for investigation and audit purposes.
implementationGuidance: Protect logs from modification or deletion. Store logs in a separate, access-controlled system. Implement integrity checking on critical logs. Restrict access to log management systems to authorised security and operations personnel.
bestPractices: Store logs in an immutable, tamper-evident system
Separate log management from production systems
Alert on any attempts to modify or delete log data

title: Administrator and Operator Logs
description: System administrator and system operator activities shall be logged and the logs protected and regularly reviewed.
purpose: To detect and investigate privileged user activities that could indicate abuse or error.
implementationGuidance: Log all administrator and privileged user activities including commands executed, systems accessed, and configuration changes made. Review these logs regularly (e.g., weekly for high-risk systems). Protect these logs from modification by the administrators themselves.
evidenceExamples: Alerts triggered by anomalous admin activity
bestPractices: Use a PAM solution to record all privileged sessions
Implement separation between who performs admin tasks and who reviews logs
Alert on admin activities outside business hours or from unusual locations

title: Clock Synchronisation
description: The clocks of all relevant information processing systems within an organisation or security domain shall be synchronised to a single reference time source.
purpose: To ensure event logs across systems can be accurately correlated during security investigations.
implementationGuidance: Configure all systems to synchronise time using NTP with a reliable, authoritative time source. Use hierarchical NTP architecture for large environments. Monitor for clock drift and NTP failures.
bestPractices: Use GPS-based stratum 1 NTP servers for highest accuracy
Monitor all systems for NTP synchronisation failures
Include time synchronisation checks in system build standards

title: Installation of Software on Operational Systems
description: Procedures shall be implemented to control the installation of software on operational systems.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised or untested software from being installed on production systems, reducing security and stability risks.
implementationGuidance: Define and enforce a policy restricting software installation to authorised packages only. Remove local administrator rights from standard users. Require change management approval for software changes on production systems. Implement application whitelisting on critical systems.
evidenceExamples: Standard user account configuration showing no local admin rights
Change tickets for software installations on production
bestPractices: Remove local administrator rights from all standard users
Use application control (whitelisting) on critical systems
Conduct quarterly software audit to identify and remove unauthorised software

title: Management of Technical Vulnerabilities
description: Information about technical vulnerabilities of information systems being used shall be obtained in a timely fashion, the organisation's exposure to such vulnerabilities evaluated, and appropriate measures taken to address the associated risk.
purpose: To ensure vulnerabilities are identified, assessed, and remediated before they can be exploited.
implementationGuidance: Implement a vulnerability management programme including regular scanning (at least weekly for internet-facing systems), vulnerability prioritisation using CVSS scores, and defined SLAs for remediation by severity. Subscribe to vulnerability intelligence feeds.
bestPractices: Implement continuous vulnerability scanning for internet-facing assets
Use CVSS scores and exploitability context to prioritise remediation
Track remediation SLAs and escalate overdue critical vulnerabilities

title: Restrictions on Software Installation
description: Rules governing the installation of software by users shall be established and implemented.
purpose: To prevent users from installing unapproved software that could introduce security risks.
implementationGuidance: Define rules about what software users may install. Remove local administrator rights where practical. Provide a corporate app store or software catalogue for approved applications. Block installation of unapproved software using technical controls.
requiredDocuments: Technical control configuration for installation restrictions
evidenceExamples: Group Policy or MDM configuration showing installation restrictions
User notification records on software installation policy
bestPractices: Remove local admin rights from all end users
Provide a self-service portal for approved software requests
Monitor for and alert on unauthorised software installation attempts

title: Information Systems Audit Controls
description: Audit requirements and activities involving verification of operational systems shall be carefully planned and agreed to minimise disruptions to business processes.
purpose: To ensure system audits are conducted without adversely impacting system availability or security.
implementationGuidance: Plan and schedule system audits in advance. Define scope, access requirements, and timing. Grant read-only access where possible. Monitor audit activity to detect any unintended impact. Document the audit scope and results.
evidenceExamples: Read-only access grant records for auditors
bestPractices: Schedule system audits during low-traffic periods
Use dedicated audit accounts with read-only access
Review and revoke audit access immediately upon audit completion

title: Network Controls
description: Networks shall be managed and controlled to protect information in systems and applications.
purpose: To ensure the network infrastructure is secured to prevent unauthorised access and protect data in transit.
implementationGuidance: Implement network security controls including firewalls, intrusion detection/prevention systems (IDS/IPS), and network monitoring. Define and enforce network security baselines. Regularly review and test network security controls.
evidenceExamples: Firewall rule documentation and review records
bestPractices: Implement network security zones with different trust levels
Use IPS in prevention mode for known attack signatures
Conduct quarterly network security reviews including firewall rule analysis

title: Security of Network Services
description: Security mechanisms, service levels, and management requirements of all network services shall be identified and included in network services agreements, whether these services are provided in-house or outsourced.
purpose: To ensure network service providers deliver adequate security controls as part of service agreements.
implementationGuidance: Define security requirements for all network services in SLAs and contracts. For outsourced network services, include security requirements, audit rights, incident notification obligations, and compliance requirements. Monitor service providers against defined SLAs.
requiredDocuments: SLA or contract terms including security clauses
evidenceExamples: Signed network service contracts with security clauses
bestPractices: Include right-to-audit clauses in network service contracts
Define and track security-specific SLAs (incident response time, uptime, patching)
Conduct annual security reviews of critical network service providers

title: Segregation in Networks
description: Groups of information services, users, and information systems shall be segregated on networks.
purpose: To limit the impact of a network security breach and control access between different network segments.
implementationGuidance: Implement network segmentation using VLANs, firewalls, or SDN. Separate networks by function (e.g., production, management, guest, PCI CDE). Control and monitor traffic between segments. Document the network segmentation architecture.
bestPractices: Apply micro-segmentation for critical systems
Implement zero-trust networking to eliminate implicit trust between segments
Test network segmentation effectiveness with penetration testing

title: Information Transfer Policies and Procedures
description: Formal transfer policies, procedures, and controls shall be in place to protect the transfer of information through the use of all types of communication facilities.
purpose: To ensure information is protected during transfer, regardless of the communication channel used.
implementationGuidance: Define procedures for secure information transfer via email, file transfer, messaging, and other channels. Specify encryption requirements for data in transit. Train users on secure transfer practices. Prohibit transfer of sensitive data via unapproved channels.
evidenceExamples: Information transfer policy published and communicated
User training records on secure data transfer
bestPractices: Enforce TLS for all email in transit
Provide secure file sharing tools to avoid shadow IT
Block transfer of sensitive data via personal email or unauthorised cloud services

title: Agreements on Information Transfer
description: Agreements shall address the secure transfer of business information between the organisation and external parties.
purpose: To establish clear obligations for information security when sharing data with external parties.
implementationGuidance: Include security requirements in agreements with external parties who send or receive business information. Specify encryption requirements, acceptable transfer methods, data handling obligations, and breach notification requirements.
evidenceExamples: Signed data transfer agreements with external parties
bestPractices: Standardise information transfer clauses for all supplier contracts
Review and update agreements when transfer requirements change
Track and manage agreements in a central repository

title: Electronic Messaging
description: Information involved in electronic messaging shall be appropriately protected.
purpose: To ensure emails and other electronic messages are protected from interception, unauthorised access, and data leakage.
implementationGuidance: Implement email encryption for sensitive communications (S/MIME, TLS). Deploy email security controls (anti-phishing, anti-spam, DLP). Define policies for use of instant messaging and other electronic communication tools for business information.
evidenceExamples: TLS enforcement evidence for email in transit
bestPractices: Enforce TLS for all email connections
Implement DMARC, SPF, and DKIM for email authentication
Deploy email DLP to prevent sensitive data leakage

title: Confidentiality or Non-disclosure Agreements
description: Requirements for confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements reflecting the organisation's needs for the protection of information shall be identified, regularly reviewed and documented.
purpose: To protect confidential information shared with employees, contractors, and third parties.
implementationGuidance: Define NDA requirements for different categories of relationships. Ensure NDAs are signed before sensitive information is shared. Review NDA templates regularly against current legal requirements. Maintain a repository of all executed NDAs.
evidenceExamples: NDA review by legal counsel records
bestPractices: Require NDA signing before any access to confidential information
Review NDA templates at least annually with legal counsel
Store executed NDAs in a centralised, searchable repository

title: Information Security Requirements Analysis and Specification
description: The information security related requirements shall be included in the requirements for new information systems or enhancements to existing information systems.
purpose: To ensure security is built into systems from inception rather than added retrospectively.
implementationGuidance: Include security requirements in functional and non-functional requirements for new systems and enhancements. Conduct threat modelling during design. Define acceptance criteria that include security requirements. Involve security team in requirements review.
requiredDocuments: System requirements templates including security sections
evidenceExamples: System requirements documents with security requirements
bestPractices: Use STRIDE threat modelling for all new system designs
Include security requirements in user stories for Agile development
Conduct security requirements review gates before development begins

title: Securing Application Services on Public Networks
description: Information involved in application services passing over public networks shall be protected from fraudulent activity, contract dispute and unauthorised disclosure and modification.
purpose: To protect data transmitted through public-facing applications from interception and tampering.
implementationGuidance: Implement TLS 1.2 or higher for all application services on public networks. Use certificate pinning where appropriate. Protect against common web attacks (injection, XSS, CSRF). Implement API security controls.
evidenceExamples: TLS configuration test results (e.g., SSL Labs A+ rating)
bestPractices: Achieve SSL Labs A+ rating for all public web applications
Implement HSTS, CSP, and other security headers
Conduct annual penetration testing of public-facing applications

title: Protecting Application Services Transactions
description: Information involved in application service transactions shall be protected to prevent incomplete transmission, mis-routing, unauthorised message alteration, unauthorised disclosure, unauthorised message duplication or replay.
purpose: To ensure the integrity, authenticity, and non-repudiation of electronic transactions.
implementationGuidance: Implement message signing and integrity checking for critical transactions. Use digital signatures for high-value transactions. Implement transaction logging and anomaly detection. Protect against replay attacks using nonces or timestamps.
bestPractices: Sign and timestamp critical transactions to ensure non-repudiation
Implement idempotency controls to prevent duplicate transactions
Log all transactions with sufficient detail for forensic investigation

title: Secure Development Policy
description: Rules for the development of software and systems shall be established and applied to developments within the organisation.
purpose: To ensure security is consistently applied throughout the software development lifecycle.
implementationGuidance: Define and enforce secure coding standards and guidelines. Require security training for developers. Integrate security testing into CI/CD pipelines (SAST, DAST, SCA). Mandate code review with security focus.
evidenceExamples: SAST/DAST integration in CI/CD pipeline evidence
bestPractices: Follow OWASP Secure Coding Practices
Integrate SAST into CI/CD so security issues fail the build
Require annual secure coding training for all developers

title: System Change Control Procedures
description: Changes to systems within the development lifecycle shall be controlled by the use of formal change control procedures.
purpose: To prevent unauthorised changes to production systems and ensure all changes are tested and approved.
implementationGuidance: Apply formal change management to all changes within the development lifecycle. Require development, test, and security sign-off before production deployment. Maintain an audit trail of all system changes.
evidenceExamples: Change tickets with full approval chain
Deployment pipeline logs showing approval gates
bestPractices: Implement automated deployment pipelines with mandatory security gates
Require separate approval from development and operations for production changes
Maintain a complete audit trail of all changes via version control and change management systems

title: Technical Review of Applications After Operating Platform Changes
description: When operating platforms are changed, business critical applications shall be reviewed and tested to ensure there is no adverse impact on organisational operations or security.
purpose: To ensure platform changes do not introduce security vulnerabilities or availability issues in dependent applications.
implementationGuidance: Define a testing process for applications when underlying platforms change (OS updates, runtime upgrades, infrastructure changes). Conduct security testing as part of platform change validation. Document test results before production deployment.
requiredDocuments: Security test results for platform changes
evidenceExamples: Test results documentation for platform upgrade projects
bestPractices: Include security testing as a mandatory gate in platform change processes
Test in lower environments before production
Create and maintain application-specific test suites for platform compatibility verification

title: Restrictions on Changes to Software Packages
description: Modifications to software packages shall be discouraged, limited to necessary changes, and all changes shall be strictly controlled.
purpose: To prevent security vulnerabilities from being introduced through uncontrolled modification of vendor software.
implementationGuidance: Define a policy on modifying vendor software packages. Prefer configuration over code modification. Where modifications are necessary, document the changes, assess security impact, and test thoroughly. Maintain modifications separately from vendor code where possible.
evidenceExamples: Documented justification for any vendor code modifications
bestPractices: Prefer vendor-supported configuration options over code modification
Document all modifications with clear justification and risk assessment
Test all vendor package modifications thoroughly before deployment

title: Secure System Engineering Principles
description: Principles for engineering secure systems shall be established, documented, maintained and applied to any information system implementation efforts.
purpose: To ensure security engineering principles are consistently applied to all system development activities.
implementationGuidance: Adopt and document security engineering principles such as least privilege, defence in depth, fail securely, separation of duties, and economy of mechanism. Train engineers on these principles. Reference them in design review processes.
requiredDocuments: Design review checklist referencing security principles
evidenceExamples: Developer training records on secure engineering
bestPractices: Reference NIST SP 800-160 or similar for security engineering guidance
Include security principle adherence in code and design reviews
Incorporate principles into architect and developer onboarding

title: Secure Development Environment
description: Organisations shall establish and appropriately protect secure development environments for system development and integration efforts that cover the entire system development lifecycle.
purpose: To prevent security breaches originating from compromised development environments.
implementationGuidance: Define security standards for development environments. Separate development from production networks. Control access to development tools and environments. Protect source code repositories and build systems. Monitor development environment security.
evidenceExamples: Developer access controls to code repositories
bestPractices: Treat developer workstations as high-risk endpoints requiring strong controls
Harden CI/CD pipeline components against attack
Monitor development environment access and activity

title: Outsourced Development
description: The organisation shall supervise and monitor the activity of outsourced system development.
purpose: To ensure software developed by third parties meets the organisation's security requirements.
implementationGuidance: Define security requirements for outsourced development in contracts. Conduct code reviews of outsourced software. Include security testing requirements. Maintain oversight of development activities and access to organisational systems.
requiredDocuments: Third-party developer contract with security clauses
Code review and acceptance testing procedures for outsourced code
evidenceExamples: Outsourced development contracts with security requirements
Code review records for third-party developed code
Security testing results for outsourced software
bestPractices: Conduct SAST and DAST on all outsourced code before acceptance
Require evidence of developer security training from outsourcers
Include right to audit and security testing clauses in outsourcing contracts

title: System Security Testing
description: Testing of security functionality shall be carried out during development.
purpose: To verify that security controls built into systems work as intended before deployment.
implementationGuidance: Integrate automated security testing (SAST, DAST, dependency scanning) into development pipelines. Conduct manual security testing for complex or high-risk systems. Document test plans, test cases, and test results.
requiredDocuments: Security test plans and test cases
bestPractices: Integrate SAST, SCA, and container scanning into CI/CD pipelines
Conduct DAST against running applications in test environments
Include security testing results as a deployment gate

title: System Acceptance Testing
description: Acceptance testing programmes and related criteria shall be established for new information systems, upgrades and new versions.
purpose: To ensure systems meet security and functional requirements before being put into production.
implementationGuidance: Define acceptance criteria that include security requirements. Conduct acceptance testing in a staging environment representative of production. Require security sign-off as part of acceptance. Document test results and any exceptions with risk acceptance.
evidenceExamples: Exception handling records with risk acceptance
bestPractices: Include security in user acceptance testing (UAT) criteria
Test in an environment that mirrors production as closely as possible
Require documented risk acceptance for any security findings deployed with known risks

title: Protection of Test Data
description: Test data shall be selected carefully, protected and controlled.
purpose: To prevent exposure of sensitive personal or business data used in testing environments.
implementationGuidance: Prohibit use of real personal data in test environments unless absolutely necessary with compensating controls. Implement data masking or anonymisation for production data used in testing. Control access to test data sets.
evidenceExamples: Data masking tool configuration and outputs
Test data approval and anonymisation records
bestPractices: Use synthetic data generation tools rather than production data copies
Implement automated data masking in data pipeline to lower environments
Treat test environments containing any sensitive data with production-level controls

title: Information Security Policy for Supplier Relationships
description: Information security requirements for mitigating the risks associated with supplier's access to the organisation's assets shall be agreed with the supplier and documented.
purpose: To ensure suppliers who access organisational assets are bound by appropriate security requirements.
implementationGuidance: Define a supplier security policy specifying requirements for suppliers accessing organisational systems, data, or premises. Include requirements in contracts. Classify suppliers by risk level and apply commensurate controls.
evidenceExamples: Supplier contracts with security requirements sections
bestPractices: Apply a risk-based approach — tier suppliers by access level and data sensitivity
Include minimum security baseline requirements for all suppliers
Review and update supplier security requirements annually

title: Addressing Security Within Supplier Agreements
description: All relevant information security requirements shall be established and agreed with each supplier that may access, process, store, communicate, or provide IT infrastructure components for, the organisation's information.
purpose: To establish contractual obligations for suppliers to protect organisational information.
implementationGuidance: Include specific security clauses in all supplier agreements covering data protection, access controls, incident notification, audit rights, subcontractor management, and compliance with applicable regulations.
requiredDocuments: Executed supplier contracts with security clauses
evidenceExamples: Signed supplier contracts with security annexes
Supplier audit rights provisions in contracts
bestPractices: Use a standard security addendum for all supplier contracts
Include GDPR Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) for processors handling personal data
Require suppliers to notify of security incidents within 24–72 hours

title: Information and Communication Technology Supply Chain
description: Agreements with suppliers shall include requirements to address the information security risks associated with information and communications technology services and product supply chain.
purpose: To ensure ICT supply chain risks are identified and managed to prevent supply chain attacks.
implementationGuidance: Include supply chain security requirements in ICT supplier contracts. Require disclosure of subcontractors. Assess the security of software components and hardware from the supply chain. Monitor for supply chain vulnerabilities.
evidenceExamples: ICT supplier contracts with supply chain clauses
Software bill of materials (SBOM) for critical software
bestPractices: Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for critical applications
Monitor open-source components for vulnerabilities using SCA tools
Assess hardware supply chain integrity for critical infrastructure components

title: Monitoring and Review of Supplier Services
description: Organisations shall regularly monitor, review and audit supplier service delivery.
purpose: To ensure suppliers continue to meet their contractual security obligations over time.
implementationGuidance: Establish a supplier monitoring programme. Conduct periodic security reviews of critical suppliers. Request and review security reports (e.g., SOC 2 reports, penetration test summaries). Maintain a supplier risk register.
bestPractices: Tier suppliers by risk and review frequency (e.g., critical suppliers annually, others every 2 years)
Request supplier SOC 2 or ISO 27001 certification where available
Track supplier security metrics in a supplier risk dashboard

title: Managing Changes to Supplier Services
description: Changes to the provision of services by suppliers, including maintaining and improving existing information security policies, procedures and controls, shall be managed, taking account of the criticality of business information, systems and processes involved and re-assessment of risks.
purpose: To ensure that changes made by suppliers do not introduce new security risks.
implementationGuidance: Require suppliers to notify of significant changes to their services, infrastructure, or security controls. Assess the security impact of supplier changes. Re-evaluate risk when suppliers make significant changes. Update contracts to reflect change notification requirements.
requiredDocuments: Change notification clause in supplier contracts
evidenceExamples: Supplier change notifications received and reviewed
Risk assessments conducted for significant supplier changes
Contract provisions requiring supplier change notification
bestPractices: Define 'significant change' in supplier contracts with clear notification timelines
Review supplier changes through your internal change management process
Update supplier risk ratings following significant changes

title: Responsibilities and Procedures
description: Management responsibilities and procedures shall be established to ensure a quick, effective and orderly response to information security incidents.
purpose: To ensure the organisation can respond effectively to security incidents and minimise their impact.
